title: Networks and IT Infrastructure
summary: IT infrastructure fundamentals: network types (LAN, MAN, WAN), topologies, Internet protocols, cloud computing, and how infrastructure supports business strategy.
content_html:
Networks and IT Infrastructure

IT infrastructure is the interconnection of basic facilities and services enabling an organisation to function and compete in the digital age. Infrastructure must evolve to support the firm's business strategy.

Evolution of IT Infrastructure

  • Mainframe era: Large centralised computers, limited processing.
  • Personal computer era: Proliferation of desktop PCs and personal software.
  • Client-server era: Desktop clients networked to servers, processing split between them.
  • Cloud computing era: Firms obtain computing power and software over the Internet. Fastest growing form.

Network Types

  • LAN (Local Area Network): Interconnects computers in a limited area (home, office, lab). High speed, privately owned.
  • MAN (Metropolitan Area Network): Geographically separated but within the same city. May be shared across organisations.
  • WAN (Wide Area Network): Covers broad geographic area (metropolitan, regional, national). Uses private or public network media. Enables business regardless of location.

Network Topologies

  • Bus: Computers on a single line.
  • Star: Central hub or computer with devices connected to it.
  • Hierarchical/Tree: Tree-like structure with parent-child relationships.

Intranet and Extranet

  • Intranet: Internal network based on web technologies allowing people within an organisation to exchange information.
  • Extranet: A network allowing selected outsiders (business partners, customers) to access authorised resources of the intranet.

Key Infrastructure Drivers

Infrastructure evolution has been driven by: developments in computer processing power, memory chips, storage devices, telecommunications hardware and software, and managed services (commoditisation of infrastructure).

Cloud Computing

Cloud computing is a model where firms obtain computing power and software applications over the Internet or other network. Benefits include scalability, reduced capital expenditure, and access to enterprise-grade capabilities without large upfront investment.

exercises.title: LAN scope
exercises.prompt: A LAN (Local Area Network) typically covers:
options: A whole country
Multiple cities in a region
A limited area such as a home, school or office building
A metropolitan city area
exercises.answer: A limited area such as a home, school or office building

exercises.title: WAN purpose
exercises.prompt: A WAN (Wide Area Network) enables organisations to:
options: Connect only within one building
Carry out business regardless of geographic location
Replace their intranet
Avoid cloud computing costs
exercises.answer: Carry out business regardless of geographic location

exercises.title: Intranet vs Extranet
exercises.prompt: An extranet differs from an intranet in that it:
options: Is used only by IT staff
Allows selected outsiders such as partners and customers to access internal resources
Is hosted on public social media
Only supports email communication
exercises.answer: Allows selected outsiders such as partners and customers to access internal resources

exercises.title: Cloud computing characteristic
exercises.prompt: Cloud computing is best described as:
options: Storing data on physical servers in the office
Obtaining computing power and software over the Internet without owning the infrastructure
A type of LAN topology
A government-owned network infrastructure
exercises.answer: Obtaining computing power and software over the Internet without owning the infrastructure

exercises.title: Star topology
options: All computers are connected in a single line
There is a central hub to which all devices connect
Computers are arranged in a tree-like hierarchy
Each computer connects directly to every other computer
exercises.answer: There is a central hub to which all devices connect

exercises.title: IT infrastructure evolution
exercises.prompt: Which era of IT infrastructure is characterised by desktop clients networked to servers with split processing?
options: Mainframe era
Personal computer era
Client-server era
Cloud computing era

exercises.title: MAN definition
options: A network limited to one floor of a building
A geographically separated network within the same city
A global wide area network
A wireless personal area network
exercises.answer: A geographically separated network within the same city

exercises.title: Infrastructure and strategy
options: Remain fixed once deployed to avoid disruption
Support the firm's business and information systems strategy, evolving with new technologies
Be managed entirely by external vendors
Focus only on hardware, not software or connectivity
exercises.answer: Support the firm's business and information systems strategy, evolving with new technologies
